speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number five, January 3, 1996, interview with Mr. Moses Goldberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were telling me about receiving communication--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --from your oldest sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And my wish was to see them. And she wanted me, I should come to Poland, to be with them, so we'd be like a family. But I had bad experience in Poland. I said, I don't want to go to Poland. We'll meet somewhere. But I don't want to go back to Poland. Because once you go to Poland, you can't get out unless they're-- at that time when they were, there was the time that no Jews was able to go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, no, I'm not going to Poland. God will help. We'll-- we'll meet somewhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was in-- in Italy for 4 and a 1/2 years. I had a lot of friends. We-- we shared everything together. I had that-- that my friend, Waserlaug, Julek Waserlaug, he went back. He-- he had an uncle there. And his uncle was writing him, come back, come back. And he went. I left-- after he left, I felt all alone. Because we were together from the concentration camp till now. We were very good friends. It was like, we had same money. No, no. It was one money. So we were like a couple. But we-- he wanted to go back. So we-- we-- we divided all our treasures, what we had, in half. And he went back. He went there, to school. He went to high schooling there. And then he wanted to leave. And he couldn't leave. He married there. And eventually, he left. He's in Israel now. But he couldn't-- he was later sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we lived in-- I lived in-- in Italy. We was-- from Cremona, I was-- I lived there for three years. It's a long time to be there. And you had to learn Italian. Because nobody understood any other language. And they only spoke Italian. English, I didn't speak. So you had to-- you are forced to learn, to teach yourself Italian. We had to go buy something or something. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we're-- after three years, the camp was liquidated. And we were sent south, to Trani, which is not far from Bari. And then we started to work on the-- to immigration. Everybody wanted to go somewhere. I already had a visa to go to Brazil. In the meantime, my-- my father's sister's daughter, her name is Fran-- her name is now Fran Laufer. She survived the war. She was in Bergen-Belsen. Also, a young girl, 18, 19 years old. And she married there, her husband, which is named Simon Laufer. Her maiden name was Fuchsbramer. It was my-- my-- her mother was my father's sister. And somewhere, I got the address. And she-- she was one of the first ones to come to America. She got some kind of a-- a push that she-- they were able to come. They came, in America, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we started to-- to write to each other. You know, at that time you had cousin, it was like a sister. So she wrote to me, I should come. And nothing-- I have everything I need.

speaker: interviewee
text: I could stay in their house. We'll be a family together. And it was very hard to go to them, because the Polish quota was closed. You could not try to come as a student. Yeshiva Torah Vodaath send me papers, to come as a student. And whenever I went to the consul, they-- they didn't want to give me the visa. They said, we have no guarantee that you're going back after you finish your studies, that you're going back to anything. So I had no other choice. We had to stay and wait in-- in Italy. Finally, Truman pass through the bill, the Displaced Person Bill, which allowed to come, the people, without the quota. So in 1949, I was-- I was able to-- I arrived in December, in America. My cousin, was-- his-- he was there at the boat. And while in Italy, I learned English. Private, I paid. There was a-- a Polish woman who was-- was giving lessons in English. And I knew I want to go to America. So I wanted to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember at the boat, when I came to America, I was-- so the American people were came to-- to pick up their relatives. So they asked me in Yiddish, do you know this and this name? And so I answered in English, yes. I know him. He'll be right out. So he said, take a look. He-- he's not even here yet, he already speaks English. But you know, I-- I am good in languages. I picked it up fast. I mean, I didn't speak perfect. But whatever I needed, I spoke English. So of course, I came to my cousin. To that-- her name was Fran Laufer. And everybody was envious of me, because I had a home.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I had a home. I had a family. I-- they have a Shabbos there, you know. And my cousin bought me a pair of-- I had tefillin from-- from Italy. But he give me all the thing. And-- and I came back to my roots, back how I was, used to be at home. Because that was the same family. And they were very good to me. We were like-- like a family. She had already, two children at that time. And for me, it was something special, because I was all alone, had nowhere to go. And it was some-- I find myself a job, slowly. I was working something, and-- and I lived with them till I got married. I came here in 19-- 1950, the end of 1950. And I was married in 1953.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name is Toby Goldberg, T-O-B-Y Goldberg. Her name-- her maiden name is Englander, the same as my mother's. We were supposed to be some kind, like, 10 generations related. But-- we didn't-- and she also comes not far from my hometown. She comes from the town of Krynica, which was also a resort town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did I meet my wife? We lived in the same neighborhood. And my brother-in-law, who you just spoke to him, and my cousin, the Laufers, they come from the same hometown.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my wife was-- was an idealist. She was in-- in-- she was in-- in hiding during the war, with her mother. And when they came out of the-- the liberation, she wanted only to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So her mother send her. Said that she was in [INAUDIBLE], in-- in a kinderheim in [INAUDIBLE]. And then she was in France, in a kinderheim.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she wanted-- only wanted to go to Israel. So her mother had no choice. Her sister came to America, the one who lives here, who you met, my brother-in-law. And her name is Naomi [? Weinstock ?]. She came to America. And my wife and her mother went to Israel. They came there in the worst time. When it was-- was just-- was just after the Independence Day. They also went on-- on-- Aliyah Bet. But she was more idealistic than I was. Because I didn't want to go. But at that time, they didn't take to Cypress anymore. They had a hard time in Israel, in the beginning. She went to school. She went to the high school there. And she went-- she was-- became a morah, a teacher there. She was young. She was-- when we married, she was 21. And I was, already, 26.

speaker: interviewer
text: So her sister wanted her to come to visit in America. She didn't see her for so many years. So she left her mother there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she is coming for a visit, for four or five weeks. She came here for a visit. And my-- my cousin, the Laufers and my brother-in-law, they were friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- they started to talk. He said, oh, I have a cousin here. So I-- I-- I met my wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met her on Passover. Which is-- and we got engaged on Lag BaOmer, which is-- which is 30 days later. And we got married after Shavuoth. So our courtship was-- was about seven weeks. And we live happily ever after till now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're very proud of your two sons.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. This is my-- my life. My kids and my grandchildren is my whole life. You see, I don't know if an American person can feel it. But a person who went through the camps, and he saw the degradation of people and everything, and-- and you came and you accomplished. To have a family, you see my pictures here. I mean, my grandchildren are-- it's-- it's an accomplishment that I cherished. And it's in spite of the Germans, who-- who wanted to-- to liquidate all the Jews. In spite of them, we-- we came back to our roots. And we became a family. That's-- that's a big achievement.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me the names of your sons and your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. My-- my oldest son is-- is named-- he's named after my father and my wife's father. His Hebrew name is [INAUDIBLE]. And his-- we call him Harry. Or we call him [INAUDIBLE], in Hebrew. His wife is-- is-- my son was born in 1955. We were married, 1953. He was born in 1955. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I then, to finish the story about my cousin, because I-- I owe them a lot. So I just want to mention that I got married from their house. And they were my-- they like, gave me away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because I had no parents, right? My sister was-- all right, I will come back to my sister later. They-- they did everything they could to-- to-- to play the role of parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they did. They-- they made-- they helped me with the wedding, did everything in there. They're very well-known now, in America. They are-- his-- he's a very big businessman, very successful businessman. I don't Know if you read 'The Jewish--' 'The Jewish Press.' And Mrs. Laufer is always in the papers. She's being honored. She was the president of Rivkah Laufer Bikur Cholim. And they're very charitable people. And there's not a week that they shouldn't be in-- in the limelight or something. They know all those big shots from Israel. And Ariel Sharon was in their house not long ago. And we are close with them too. We attend all the simchas together. And we call each other. We see each other often. She's like-- I'll-- she's my age. But I feel like-- like a sister to me. And we are very close together. OK, now we'll go back to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell me about your sister. When did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. My sister stayed in Poland. And she also wanted to-- I mean, they want to go to Israel. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And through the Joint, they were able to go to France-- this was already after the independence-- and thereafter, Israel. Now, when they came to Israel, I was still single. At that time it was very expensive, the trip. The planes weren't going yet like this, like now. And the boats, they were-- it took a long time. I had a job. I just couldn't afford to go to Israel. And I said, I'll wait till I get married, we'll go. Then, you know, when you get married, you have children. And it's expensive, everything. We couldn't go. In 1960-- I got married in 1953. In 1960, I was-- I was already working, at that time, for a big company who manufactures men's clothing, men's sport jackets and slacks. And there were German Jews. I was the only shomer Shabbos in that whole place. And they tolerated. They paid for all my Jewish holidays. Fridays, it came two o'clock, they chase me out of the place, I should go home already. They were very nice to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, well, you know-- we were talking about my history. He says, you have a sister in Israel. You didn't see her yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I would love to go. But I just can't afford right now. My kids, you know, the Yeshivas and that. He says, I'll help you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he paid for my ticket. And I spent four weeks in Israel. He paid me for my ticket. He paid for my salaries. My wife went to the country with the children. I went to Israel. So when I arrived to Israel, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my sister was supposed to be at the airport. But what happened, that my plane arrived two hours before.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they didn't have no phones to call at that time. And she lived in a small town. And I was waiting and waiting, and at the airport. My friend that-- who was with me, came. He lives in Nazareth, the one who went to Poland. He came to the airport. And we were waiting. And I would see my sister in the street, I wouldn't recognize her. She was-- She has a son who is four years younger than I am. And she was like a mother to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my-- my niece came with her. And she says, here, this is your brother. And you know, the first time after-- after-- from 1938, till-- till 19-- till 1960, that's many years. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: You-- but I-- we had a beautiful time. I spent four weeks there. And my sister was so attached to me that she never let me go anywhere. And she didn't want to go anywhere. So I had to stay, most of the time, with her. And we talked. We had so much to talk about, about the family. Because nobody else was alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then after four weeks, I came-- and my sister was trying to-- to make me heavy, to make me fat, to get me to eat. You know, like the-- all the mamas are? I used to eat the laban in her house. You know laban, what this is? You know what laban is? And-- it's like a sour milk, something.

speaker: interviewee
text: She used to put-- she used to put fat things instead, like sour cream, to make it fat. Because I used to go to Jerusalem. And I used to eat the same thing. And I came back. I said, you know, it doesn't taste the same thing. Then she tells me. My kids didn't recognize me when I came home. I was like this. I was all blown up. That's how she-- she fed me. I had to go on a diet here.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war, what was your relationship with God?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war-- you see, after the war, people were like-- [? half ?] [? scared. ?] You know? What I mean, even the most religious people who are now, they were like-- it's-- they-- they were left with-- with-- with nothing. They had to come back to it slowly. It took me about a few, three months. And-- and I came back to the-- because you see, my pictures are-- I'm walking around without a hats, right after the war. And-- and we tried to eat kosher. But it wasn't-- it was very hard. But then, after-- after a while, you came back to-- to-- to your own-- own belief. And-- and I had my pair of tefillin. I used to go to daven. And I-- I-- I led a Jewish Orthodox life, as much as I could. Here in America it wasn't-- it was easy. But in camps and after the war, it was-- we had a kosher kitchen. And a different kitchen. So some people who wanted to eat kosher belonged to the kosher kitchen. So it was-- wasn't so bad. Outside, you couldn't eat too much, unless you ate something you know, like sodas or something, ice cream.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever tell your children about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till the Schindler's movie, I wasn't talking at all. Then when the book-- when my book came out, I gave-- gave them each, my children, a book. And they read it. And once in awhile, I'll tell them the story. But this-- this will be like, a-- a story for them to know exactly what happened, the-- the video here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that you will sit and watch it with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I will explain it to them, too. If-- I would. But my oldest son is very interested in it. And so is my youngest son. My youngest son maybe is-- he's young. He's young. He is more emotional. My-- my older son, we understand each other very well too. So I will-- I would like-- like that they should see that-- that video. And they will see what-- what the Jewish people had to go through here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a special message to the world and to future generations about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have. I hope-- I hope it never repeats, the history never repeats. And there are signs. You know, people-- there are people who-- who deny, that what-- nothing happened. There was no concentration camp. There was no camp. As we-- as long as we are alive, we could-- we could deny-- we could tell them it's not true.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what's going to be later? Maybe those-- those videos will help anything. Because there are people who deny it, that nothing happened like this. And you speak to a German. I had met somebody in Germany. He says, well, we didn't know anything about it. Which is not true, we didn't know for-- the war was from 1939 to '45. For five years they didn't know what's going on? They didn't-- they didn't want to know. Do you have one message for the world, some words to say about Oskar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- I would like to say about Oskar Schindler, that he was a righteous man. He wanted-- he wanted to save the lives. I don't know what brought him to it. But he had a heart of gold. He risk his own life to save the Jews, especially the Jews in Brunnlitz. He risk his life, not once. He was jailed, already too, not once or twice, everything, because he helped the Jews. And I hope there will be more people would be-- I only hoped there would have been more people like him. It could have saved more-- more lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to thank you so much for sharing your story with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome. I am glad that I was able to tell the story. And I hope people will learn anything from it, what we went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, again.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my picture, when I was about five years old. It was in the winter in Stary Sacz. On the left is my friend, [INAUDIBLE]. I'm in the middle. And my brother, Benek Goldberg, is behind me. We are on a sled in the wintertime, in Stary Sacz. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This picture is from my sister's wedding. Her name was Chana. The wedding was in 1938. On the left is my oldest sister, Sarah, is my father-- [? bless ?] [? memory-- ?] my brother, Samuel, my brother, Benek, my sister, Lotti, and my brother, Baruch. And all on the bottom, that's me. I was about 12 years old. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of my-- my sister's wedding, Chana. And it's a family picture. I'm there, between my dear Chana, and my other sister. You could see me just standing there with the cap. And the rest is all my family, some aunts and uncles. And my sister, Sarah, is all the way on the-- on the end table, on the left. My brother, Baruch, is in the hat. Then there's Samuel. And then there's Benek. The rest is cousins and uncles. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is my sister, Mala. She doesn't appear in any of the wedding pictures because she wasn't there. She was left at home to mind the business. She was my-- one of my youngest sisters. And she never went with all the other people, of Kiddush HaShem. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture is of myself, in the happy times. This picture was taken in 1945, just a few months after the liberation. And I was wearing the uniform that I was wearing in Brunnlitz and Plaszow. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This picture is of myself in-- in Italy, in Cremona. I think it was made in 1947. And I was young and happy. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture is made in camp Cremona, in the winter. We are waiting in the line to get our lunch.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And who were the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, those are my friends from the-- from the same room that we live together. One name is [INAUDIBLE]. The others, I don't remember their name anymore. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This postcard was sent to me by my sister from Poland, after she found out that I'm alive and I'm in Italy. This was the happiest day of my life, when I got the postcard from my sister. I keep it as a memento. Unfortunate, she's not here anymore. But this stays with me all my life. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture is made in France. I think it was in 1948. This is my-- on the left is my sister, Sarah, her son, [? Moshe, ?] her daughter, [? Rachel, ?] and my brother-in-law, [? Ari. ?] [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] OK, this picture is my pride and joy, as I survived the war and was able to-- to [NON-ENGLISH], to-- to-- to [NON-ENGLISH] the bar mitzvah of my second grandchild. I'll-- I'll name the names. On the left, the little boy, is Yankee Goldberg. Standing next to him is his father, my son, Eli Goldberg. Sitting is his wife, [INAUDIBLE] Goldberg. Standing is Riva Goldberg, the daughter of [INAUDIBLE] Goldberg, who was sitting next to her. My daughter-in-law, which is like a daughter to me, [? Susie ?] Goldberg. And that's-- and that's myself, a little bit [YIDDISH] in the middle. Next to myself is the bar mitzvah boy, Yankee Goldberg. And sitting on the right is my lovely wife, Toby Goldberg. My little-- my youngest granddaughter, Malka. Then is [? David ?] Goldberg, in the hat, next to the bar mitzvah boy. His sister, Rachel Goldberg. And a little Tzvika Goldberg, the son of Eli. And Motti Goldberg. This is my Motti. And I will-- I'll also mention that [INAUDIBLE] is-- is to me is my daughter, too. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK, this picture is my-- my wife and myself. As you can look at the picture, you see how happy we are. We are married 42 years, till my [INAUDIBLE]. And we hope we-- our life will be as good in the future as it was till now. Well, this picture is of the Beit Midrash in Stary Sacz, which was packed with people before the war. Unfortunately, standing empty now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture was taken recently?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This picture was taken in 1985. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This one is a smicha for my son's [NON-ENGLISH]. He-- he got it with-- with very big honors. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] OK. This is a smicha for my youngest son, Eli [INAUDIBLE], which we are very proud of him. He is a lovely son. And he's doing some other work, not rabbinical work. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Oh, this is a-- a copy of one of the famous Schindler's lists, which I received through [INAUDIBLE]. My name is-- appears on number 311, Goldberg, Moses.
